title: Fix semantic-emoji mismatch: 'salad' maps to canned-goods emoji
description: User-reported: typing "salad" into the add bar produces 🥫 (canned/tin glyph), which reads as odd/wrong for a salad item. Root cause traced in this session: EmojiTable (Sources/Services/EmojiTable.swift) has no keyword entry for the bare word "salad" (only "salad cream" -> 🥫 in tools/emoji_supplement.txt line 753, which cannot match "salad" alone via EmojiTable.match's `lower.contains(keyword)` check since the input is shorter than that multi-word keyword). So EmojiTable.match("salad") returns nil and the Emoji.forName cascade (Sources/Services/Emoji.swift) falls through to stage 2, SemanticEmoji.match (Sources/Services/SemanticEmoji.swift), which uses NLEmbedding on-device word vectors to find the nearest "anchor" word among a curated anchor list (SemanticEmoji.swift ~line 44-61) and use its glyph. "salad" is not itself an anchor. The anchor list includes several condiment/canned-goods anchors that all map to the same 🥫 glyph: "vinegar" -> 🥫, "sauce" -> 🥫, "ketchup" -> 🥫, "mustard" -> 🥫 (line 60-61). Apple's on-device embedding apparently places "salad" closer to one of these condiment anchors than to any produce/vegetable anchor (e.g. "tomato" -> 🍅, "lettuce" if present), likely because of co-occurrence with "salad dressing"/"salad sauce" in the embedding's training corpus, so nearest-anchor search lands on a canned-goods glyph instead of something produce-like. Two independent angles to fix, either or both: (1) add "salad" as an explicit EmojiTable keyword (e.g. -> 🥗, the existing salad-bowl emoji, which the emoji set already has) so stage 1 short-circuits before the semantic fallback ever runs — this is the fast, precise fix and matches how other common single-word items are handled; (2) reconsider whether condiment anchors like vinegar/sauce/ketchup/mustard sharing one 🥫 glyph in the anchor list is pulling too many unrelated near words toward canned-goods, or whether SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD (SemanticEmoji.swift ~line 9, MIN_SIMILARITY-style constant gating the accept threshold) is too permissive for this case. Follow the repo's Emoji pipeline docs in CLAUDE.md (tools/gen_emoji.py -> Sources/Services/EmojiTable.swift generator, then re-run tools/audit_coverage.swift against tools/corpus/*.txt) if the fix goes the EmojiTable-keyword route.

title: Dismiss-keyboard button should clear the add-bar text + suggestions
description: User-reported (with screenshot showing "Salad" typed with 4 typeahead suggestions still visible below): pressing the down-chevron "dismiss keyboard" button in the add bar (Sources/Views/AddBar.swift lines 68-81, accessibility id A11yID.AddBar.dismissKeyboard) currently only does `focused.wrappedValue = false` + a soft haptic (line 69-71) — it does NOT clear the typed `text`. Since `text` is a `@Binding` owned by the parent (ShoppingListView.swift ~line 123, passed in as `$draft`) and the live suggestion stack (`suggestions: liveSuggestions` ~line 124) is presumably derived from that same draft text, leaving `text` populated after dismissal leaves the stale typeahead suggestion buttons floating on screen with no keyboard, which reads as broken/orphaned UI (see screenshot: "Salad", "Salad Bag", "Salad Cream", "Salad Dressing" suggestion rows still shown under an empty-list state with keyboard gone). User's stated intent: tapping the keyboard-dismiss chevron is an explicit "cancel/dismiss the input" gesture, not just "put the keyboard away" — so it should also clear the draft text (and thus, via the existing derivation, the suggestions disappear for free without needing to touch suggestion-computation logic separately). Likely fix: in the button action closure (AddBar.swift line 69-71), also reset `text = ""` (mind that `text` is a `@Binding<String>`, so this is `text.wrappedValue = ""` if using the property-wrapper form, or plain `text = ""` inside the View body context) before/alongside `focused.wrappedValue = false`. Worth checking whether any UITest (e.g. AddItemFlowTests.swift, SuggestionsFlowTests.swift) asserts today that dismissing the keyboard preserves the typed text — if so that assertion's expectation would need to flip. Also worth checking `submit()` (AddBar.swift line 114-117) and the `+` button (line 83-98) are unaffected, since this change should be scoped only to the explicit dismiss-keyboard chevron, not the add/submit paths which intentionally keep text/focus behavior as-is.

title: Got-it row: replace green checkmark with up-arrow to signal undo; remove redundant per-item undo affordance
description:
User-reported (voice-dictated, 3 screenshots): screenshot 1 shows the to-get list ("Ginger Ale", "Carpet cleaner") with a "🥫 Salad ⬑" row floating just above the add bar/keyboard (this is the existing live-suggestion row from AddBar.swift, using the "arrow.up.left" SF Symbol at Sources/Views/AddBar.swift line 32, styled via .basketCard()). Screenshot 2 shows the same screen after "Salad" was checked off: it now sits in the "Got it" section, struck through, with a filled green circle + white checkmark on the right (CheckCircle, Sources/Views/ItemRow.swift lines 212-238, using SF Symbol "checkmark" at line 229, filled with Theme.leaf). Screenshot 3 is a tight crop isolating just the "🥫 Salad ⬑" suggestion row and the add-bar controls (keyboard-dismiss icon + green plus button) — i.e. zooming in specifically on the up-left-arrow glyph.

User's literal ask (dictated, possibly slightly garbled per known dictation-typo patterns — flagged, not resolved, here): "I think I'd like to remove the functionality to undo a single item that has been marked as done. People can just untick the item in the list below if they want to. There's no need to have both an undo button and an uncheckable thing. If anything, let's remove the part that is specifically about the example above, where the salad has an individual sort of typeahead fix. It's like you press this button, and it will undo the done. I think let's just replace the symbol. In the Got It list, there is a green check. Maybe what we could do is replace that green check with the arrow pointing up. That way, we can reuse the same symbol, which speaks to undo this action, but doesn't require a whole other part of the UI. I just find that the UI becomes a bit cluttered if you also include having an undo section above the keyboard."

Interpreted intent: (1) the "Got it" section's green checkmark circle (CheckCircle in ItemRow.swift) should have its glyph changed from "checkmark" to an up-arrow SF Symbol (e.g. "arrow.up" or reusing the exact "arrow.up.left" glyph already used for AddBar suggestions at AddBar.swift line 32), so the same tap-to-untoggle affordance (already wired: CheckCircle onTapGesture -> onToggle, ItemRow.swift line 105; the row-level tap and accessibility action also call onToggle for got-it rows, lines 96-97, 116) visually reads as "undo" via the arrow rather than looking like a static checkmark; (2) some OTHER, separate "undo button" that appears "above the keyboard" for a single just-checked item should be REMOVED as redundant, since untapping the Got-it row already does the same thing.

Important caveat for the conversion/scoping step: a targeted code search this session (ItemRow.swift, ShoppingListView.swift, AddBar.swift) found NO distinct single-item "undo button above the keyboard" separate from (a) the CheckCircle tap-to-untoggle (already existing, keep) and (b) the existing "Cleared N items — Undo" ClearToast (Sources/Views/ClearToast.swift), which is a BATCH undo for "Clear all" in the Got-it section, unrelated to a single item. It's possible the user is (a) referring to the AddBar suggestion row itself (the "🥫 Salad ⬑" row visible in all 3 screenshots, which is just the live-typeahead suggestion for re-adding a known item by name, not an undo control) and conflating/misremembering it as an "undo the done" button, or (b) describing a UI element from a different build/mockup not currently in this codebase, or (c) the dictation garbled a description of something that does map to real code but wasn't identified here. This ambiguity should be clarified during implementation-harness-convert-ideas (or with the user directly) rather than guessed at — do not assume which reading is correct when scoping the eventual task. What IS unambiguous and directly actionable: swap CheckCircle's checkmark glyph for an up-arrow glyph in the Got-it (isChecked) state (Sources/Views/ItemRow.swift, CheckCircle struct, line 229 "Image(systemName: \"checkmark\")"), and confirm/remove whichever separate affordance the user means once identified. Also check Tests/SnapshotTests.swift and any UITest asserting on the check/checkmark glyph or its accessibility label (e.g. TapPrecisionTests.swift references A11yID.QuantityEditor and A11yID.ItemRow.check but not the SF Symbol itself, so likely unaffected) once the glyph changes.

title: Shorten the 'Cleared N items' undo toast duration (halve it)
description: User-reported: after "Clear all" in the Got-it section, the "Cleared N items — Undo" toast (Sources/Views/ClearToast.swift) stays visible too long — user perceives it as roughly 8 seconds and wants it cut to about half that. Actual current value found in code: Sources/Services/TestHooks.swift line 45, `static var clearToastDuration: TimeInterval { disableAnimations ? 3.0 : 5.0 }` — i.e. 5.0s in normal app use (the `disableAnimations` / UI-testing branch is a separate, already-shortened 3.0s used only under automated UI tests, TestHooks.disableAnimations gated by the -uiTestingDisableAnimations launch flag, and should probably stay untouched since UI tests likely time against that constant). This constant is consumed at Sources/Views/ShoppingListView.swift line 396: `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + TestHooks.clearToastDuration) { ... }`, which is the auto-dismiss timer that actually deletes the cleared items once the toast expires (see ShoppingListView.swift ~line 380-416, `pendingClearIDs` / `undoClear()` — items arent actually deleted until this timer fires, so shortening the toast also shortens the undo window). User's ask is specifically to roughly halve the real-world 5.0s value (their subjective "8 seconds" is likely just an overestimate of the actual 5.0s, not a literal separate figure) — so halving the 5.0 would land around 2.5s. Note this duration doubles as the undo-window length, not just toast visibility, so shortening it does trade off how long a user has to tap "Undo" after a mis-tap on Clear all.

title: Got-it section flickers back for a beat when the Clear-all undo toast expires
description:
User-reported bug: check an item off (moves to "Got it" section) -> tap "Clear All" -> "Cleared 1 item" undo toast appears and the Got-it section disappears immediately -> when the undo toast times out and disappears on its own, the Got-it section briefly REAPPEARS for "about half a beat" and then immediately disappears again. Expected: the Got-it section should just stay gone once cleared; it should never flash back.

Root cause found in this session, in Sources/Views/ShoppingListView.swift: `recentlyGot` (line 59-62) is computed as `ListLogic.recentlyGot(items, now: now, ttl: gotTTL).filter { !pendingClearIDs.contains($0.persistentModelID) }` — i.e. an item only vanishes from the Got-it section by being in the `pendingClearIDs` buffer (set immediately on "Clear all" tap, in `clearGot()` line 383-400) OR by actually being deleted from the SwiftData-backed `items` `@Query` (line 8). The undo-window expiry handler `commitClear()` (line 404-412) does, in this order: (1) `context.delete(item)` for each buffered item, (2) `pendingClearIDs.removeAll()`, (3) fade out the toast. The bug: `pendingClearIDs.removeAll()` (step 2) runs synchronously right after issuing the SwiftData deletes (step 1), but the `@Query items` array (line 8, `@Query(sort: \\.createdAt, order: .reverse) private var items`) does not necessarily refresh synchronously in the same view-update pass that `pendingClearIDs` changes in — SwiftData `@Query` re-fetches tend to land on a subsequent runloop/render pass after `context.delete`. So there's a transient frame where `pendingClearIDs` is already empty (no longer filtering the items out) but `items` still contains the not-yet-actually-removed deleted objects, so `recentlyGot` briefly includes them again -> the Got-it section flashes back into existence -> then the next render (once `items` catches up to the deletion) removes them again -> section disappears for good. This matches the user's "half a beat" description exactly.

Likely fix directions (not decided here, just noted as options for the eventual task): (a) don't call `pendingClearIDs.removeAll()` in `commitClear()` at all — since the items are now actually deleted, `pendingClearIDs` only needs to be cleared to keep the Set from growing unboundedly across repeated clear cycles, so it could be cleared lazily (e.g. at the START of the next `clearGot()` call, or filtered down to ids still present in `items`) rather than synchronously with the delete; (b) wrap the delete + removeAll in a single `withAppAnimation` transaction/explicit `Transaction` so SwiftData's query refresh and the `pendingClearIDs` state change are coalesced into one render pass rather than two; (c) reorder so `showClearToast = false` / any UI-affecting state settles only after confirming `items` no longer contains the cleared ids. Related state also worth checking during the fix: `clearToken` (line 34, 394-397) which guards against a stale timer firing after a newer clear/undo — shouldn't interact with this bug directly but is adjacent code in the same function. Also relevant: `undoClear()` (line 416-424) does the mirror-image `pendingClearIDs.removeAll()` on the undo (tap) path, but there deletion never happened, so no equivalent flicker is expected there — only the auto-expiry path (`commitClear`) actually deletes data, which is why only that path shows the bug.

title: Reduce live add-bar suggestion count from 4 to 3
description: User-reported (with screenshot showing 4 suggestion rows floating above the add bar: "Salad", "Ginger Ale", "Carpet cleaner", "Tabasco"): the "usual"/typeahead suggestion stack above the add-bar text field currently shows up to 4 items at once; user would like it reduced to 3. Root cause / exact location found in this session: `Sources/Services/Suggestions.swift` line 16, `static let combinedMax = 4` — this single constant caps both suggestion paths in that file (used via `.prefix(combinedMax)` at line 56 and again at line 84, one path for prefix/dictionary matches, the other for a blended prefix+frequency+recency score per `KnownItem`, see the scoring function around lines 87-95). Likely fix is a one-line change: `combinedMax = 4` -> `combinedMax = 3`. Consumed by `AddBar.swift`'s `suggestions` array (rendered as a `ForEach` stack, Sources/Views/AddBar.swift lines 19-47) via `liveSuggestions` computed in `ShoppingListView.swift` (line 124 passes `suggestions: liveSuggestions`). Worth checking Tests/ or UITests (e.g. SuggestionsFlowTests.swift, UsualsFlowTests.swift) and Tests/SnapshotTests.swift for any test asserting exactly 4 suggestions or a hardcoded count that would need updating alongside the constant change.

title: ClearedCelebration ('All done!' 🎉) still clips out on dismiss — second attempt needed
description:
User-reported (voice-dictated, "to-da emoji" = dictation garble for "ta-da"/celebration 🎉): the full-screen "All done!" celebration that plays when the last to-get item is checked off bursts/fades IN nicely over ~0.2s, but on the way OUT it "clips out" rather than fading gracefully — user explicitly says "I don't really know how else to describe this... the animation doesnt appear to be fading out gracefully."

THIS IS A REPEAT OF A PREVIOUSLY FAILED FIX ATTEMPT — full history found via git log this session:
- a955247 — celebration feature originally added.
- 2318dad / 975358e — "Don't overlap the 'All done' celebration with the empty state" (added the `!listIsEmpty` guard still present at ShoppingListView.swift line 110).
- 496411d — "T002: fix ClearedCelebration dismiss animation curve": changed the dismiss `withAnimation` from `.easeIn(duration: 0.4)` to `.easeOut(duration: 0.5)` in `celebrateCleared()`s dismiss timer (was `Sources/Views/ShoppingListView.swift`, function since renamed/same location ~line 372-375).
- d112d01 — **that fix was REJECTED by the user and the task marked failed**, with the exact user quote preserved in the commit message: "When I click through all of my basket, the 'All Done' appears fading in, but it still clips out. It doesn't actually fade out like I asked for." — i.e. an easing-curve-only tweak did not solve it.
- The T002 backlog id was later reassigned to an unrelated task ("UI-test launch arguments accumulate across relaunches", now status "done") after the celebration-animation T002 failed, so there is currently NO open backlog task tracking this bug — this idea capture is the first re-opening of it.
- Current code (`git show HEAD:Sources/Views/ShoppingListView.swift`) has the dismiss animation back at `.easeIn(duration: 0.4)` (i.e. the failed T002 change was effectively reverted/never landed permanently), confirming the bug is exactly where it was before that attempt.

Current relevant code, read this session:
- `Sources/Views/ShoppingListView.swift` line 110-113: outer visibility is `if celebrating && !listIsEmpty { ClearedCelebration(reduceMotion: reduceMotion).transition(.opacity) }` inside the screen's root `ZStack`.
- `celebrateCleared()` (~line 365-376): shows via `withAppAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.3)) { celebrating = true }`; after `TestHooks.celebrationDuration` (1.6s in normal use, `nil`/suppressed under UI testing — `Sources/Services/TestHooks.swift` line 36) fires `withAppAnimation(.easeIn(duration: 0.4)) { celebrating = false }` to dismiss. This single boolean flip is what drives the SwiftUI `.transition(.opacity)` removal of the whole `ClearedCelebration` subtree.
- `Sources/Views/ClearedCelebration.swift`: internally has its OWN independent `@State private var shown = false` that is set true exactly once in `.onAppear` (lines 30-35, spring or easeIn depending on `reduceMotion`) and animates the 🎉 `Text` (line 18-22, `.scaleEffect(shown ? 1 : 0.4).opacity(shown ? 1 : 0)`) and the "All done!" label (line 24-28, `.opacity(shown ? 1 : 0)`) INDEPENDENTLY of the outer container's opacity transition. `shown` is NEVER reset back to `false` anywhere — there is no mirrored "unshow" animation on dismiss, so the inner content's own scale/opacity state is already fully "shown" (1.0) at the moment the outer container starts fading via its `.transition(.opacity)`; the inner view has no exit-side counterpart to the entrance flourish.
- Also present in the same `ZStack`: `SparkleBurst(color:..., count: 16, radius: 130)` (line 14-17, only when `!reduceMotion`) — a separate component (`Sources/Views/SparkleBurst.swift`) with its OWN independent `@State private var animate = false`, fired once via `.onAppear` over `.easeOut(duration: 0.55)`, moving/fading 16 sparkle glyphs outward. This burst plays out fully within the first ~0.55s after the celebration appears and has no relationship to the later dismiss (celebrationDuration is 1.6s, so the burst is long finished by the time dismiss begins) — probably not the cause, but is nested inside the same view being faded and worth ruling out explicitly rather than assuming.

Suspects for why the exit reads as a "clip" rather than a graceful fade (not resolved/decided here — for the eventual fix to investigate): (1) the OUTER `.transition(.opacity)` + `withAppAnimation` state-flip approach may not be reliably interpolating for this view — worth checking whether `withAppAnimation` (the house wrapper mentioned in root CLAUDE.md, "prefer withAppAnimation ... over bare withAnimation/.animation so animations stay disable-able under test") has any short-circuit under certain conditions (e.g. `TestHooks.disableAnimations`) that could make production builds behave differently than expected — should confirm this bug reproduces outside UI-testing/animation-disabled builds specifically since the T002 rejection happened via manual device use, not automated tests; (2) `celebrating && !listIsEmpty` is a COMPOUND condition — if `listIsEmpty` (line 65, `toGet.isEmpty && recentlyGot.isEmpty`) happens to flip during the dismiss window (e.g. the "Got it" section's 1-hour TTL, or some other concurrent state change) the view could be yanked out of the tree via the `!listIsEmpty` half of the condition WITHOUT its opacity transition playing at all, which would look exactly like an abrupt "clip" rather than the intended `.easeIn`/`.easeOut` fade — worth checking if this compound-condition removal path bypasses the transition in practice, vs. the simple `celebrating` flip; (3) the inner `shown`-driven content has no exit animation at all (as noted above) — even if the outer opacity fade IS working, the inner Text/label content's own transform states never animate back down, so the visual result of "outer view fading while inner content stays pinned at full scale/opacity" may itself look like a hard clip rather than a graceful shrink-and-fade, especially if timing/curve mismatches make the outer fade look abrupt in the final ~10-20% where opacity crosses low thresholds quickly under `.easeIn`. Any real fix should be manually verified on-device/simulator by an agent actually watching the animation play (per this repo's UI-change verification convention), not just inferred from code, since the previous attempt (T002) looked plausible on paper and was still rejected as not fixing the actual visual problem.
